topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes: "Congratulations on your published work, [Wrong Name]." My blood immediately began to boil. I reread the text, my jaw tightening so hard I could feel the muscles strain in my neck. This wasn't a mistake; this was a calculated theft. I stood up so abruptly my chair scraped loudly across the hardwood floor. Every nerve ending felt electrified, and I paced the small apartment, hands clenched into fists so tight my knuckles were white. I needed to find the editor, needed to confront this blatant deception, needed to make some kind of noise that would shake this entire publishing house.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in short, sharp gasps. The realization hit me with the force of a physical impact, sending a wave of hot, sickening anger washing over me. It was impossible. My unique analysis, the months of painstaking research, the very voice I had poured into those pages—it was all attributed to some stranger. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound born of pure indignation. I slammed my laptop shut, the sharp crack echoing in the quiet room. I walked to the window, gripping the sill until my fingers ached. The city lights blurred, but all I could see was the injustice, the sheer audacity of the impersonation. I felt a desperate need to shatter something, anything, to release the pressure building behind my eyes.

[story 3]

When I finally read the notification, the air seemed to evacuate the room. A deep, simmering heat started rising from my chest, making my skin prickle. I leaned back in my chair, not moving, just letting the weight of the situation settle. My hands, resting on the desk, began to tremble almost imperceptibly. I looked at the name—a name that wasn't mine—and a savage sense of betrayal washed over me. I pushed my chair back violently, the screech of metal on wood demanding attention. I began to pace the length of the room, my steps quick and sharp, each stride fueled by a relentless, burning resentment. I needed retribution. I needed to find out who had pulled this stunt and ensure they understood the depth of their transgression.
